coredata.dc:title: An environmental evaluation of carbonaceous aerosols in PM10 at micro- and nano-scale levels reveals the formation of carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Rabha, Shahadev
Saikia, Binoy K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbonaceous aerosols play significant roles in air quality and the climate; their oxidation at the nano-scale level may possibly increase the reactivity and toxicity of atmospheric particulates. In the present study, a laboratory experiment on the atmospheric carbonaceous aerosol was done by using H2O2 as an oxidizing agent. An extensive study made with advanced analytical tools revealed the formation of photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles (carbon nanodots) in the carbonaceous aerosol. The carbon nanoparticles are mostly at the sp2 hybridization state and contain various surface functional groups such as carboxyl and carbonyl groups. The properties of these carbon nanoparticles resemble the engineered carbon nanoparticles such as carbon dots (CDs). The carbon nanoparticles, mainly less than 10 nm, are composed of carbon nanocrystals containing a few other elements such as Ca and Fe. Fluorescence spectroscopy revealed the characteristic excitation-dependent emission spectra of blue fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. The results indicate the presence of characteristic carbon nanoparticles in the carbonaceous aerosol in PM10, opening a new road for predicting environmental processes occurring in the atmospheric environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonaceous aerosol
Carbon nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Toxicity
Climate impact

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Environmental Chemistry (including Persistent Organic Pollutants and Dioxins)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible oxidation of atmospheric carbon nanoparticles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electron beam analysis of filtrate-A reveals soot particles with characteristic chain-like aggregates freshly emitted from heterogeneous sources. (a) FE-SEM image showing the morphology of soot particles, (b) HR-TEM image and (c) under high-resolution soot particle showing disorganized core and outer graphitic arrangement.

(a) HR-TEM analysis of filtrate-B showing dispersed carbon nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm and corresponding SAED pattern, (b) TEM image of the yellow line showing the lattice fringes of 0.220 nm which corresponds to (110) planes of graphite, (c) EDS spectra showing a high percentage of carbon content and (d) particle size distribution of the filtrate-B, (e–f) EDS elemental mapping for carbon and oxygen (also see Fig. S5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) XPS spectra of C1s showing C–C (sp2 carbon), C–O, and O–CO bondings. c) O1s spectra showing C–O and CO bondings and (d) UV–visible spectrum with an absorption peak at 235 nm (Abs.); Fluorescence (PL) showing excitation-dependence emission spectra, and bright blue photoluminescence of the sample under a UV lamp at 360 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram showing the possible reaction of carbon nanoparticles with atmospheric H2O2 to form surface-functionalized hydrophilic carbon nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: An environmental evaluation of carbonaceous aerosols in PM10 at micro- and nano-scale levels reveals the formation of carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology, Jorhat, 785006, Assam, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology, Jorhat, 785006, Assam, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group
CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonaceous aerosols play significant roles in air quality and the climate; their oxidation at the nano-scale level may possibly increase the reactivity and toxicity of atmospheric particulates. In the present study, a laboratory experiment on the atmospheric carbonaceous aerosol was done by using H2O2 as an oxidizing agent. An extensive study made with advanced analytical tools revealed the formation of photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles (carbon nanodots) in the carbonaceous aerosol. The carbon nanoparticles are mostly at the sp2 hybridization state and contain various surface functional groups such as carboxyl and carbonyl groups. The properties of these carbon nanoparticles resemble the engineered carbon nanoparticles such as carbon dots (CDs). The carbon nanoparticles, mainly less than 10 nm, are composed of carbon nanocrystals containing a few other elements such as Ca and Fe. Fluorescence spectroscopy revealed the characteristic excitation-dependent emission spectra of blue fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. The results indicate the presence of characteristic carbon nanoparticles in the carbonaceous aerosol in PM10, opening a new road for predicting environmental processes occurring in the atmospheric environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Atmospheric carbonaceous aerosols have drawn increasing attention in scientific research due to their implications for health and environment as well as their complex physicochemical properties (Pope et al., 2009; Bond et al., 2013). The carbonaceous aerosol is a constituent of the atmospheric aerosol or particulate matter (PM) consisting of elemental carbon (EC) or black carbon (BC), organic carbon (OC), and carbonates containing carbon-carbon bonds. Anthropogenic incomplete combustion of biomass and fossil fuels are considered as the main source of carbonaceous aerosol. Around 20–60 percent mass of urban particulate pollutants (PM2.5 and PM10) is contributed by carbonaceous aerosols, with total carbon content 45–90% of mass concentration and particle size ranging from 15 to 100 nm depending upon their source of emission (Huang et al., 2018; Long et al., 2013). Most of the combustion emissions possess graphitic-like structured EC- and OC-containing organic compounds which have the susceptibility to form aggregates with branched chains of clustered, fractal-like carbonaceous particles. The carbonaceous aerosol aggregated from combustion sources may form various types of carbon nanoparticles such as fullerenes and carbon nanotube in the atmosphere (Murr and Guerrero, 2006; Saikia et al., 2016).

The formation of carbon nanoparticles in combustion emissions may be an important reason for the health and climatic implications of aerosols. The pure forms of carbon nanoparticles have low toxicity but their exposure in high concentration may result in inflammation, hypersensitivity, and cytotoxicity (Morimoto et al., 2013; Lategan et al., 2018). The functionalized carbon nanoparticles are highly hydrophilic, have high cell permeability, and can induce oxidative stress (Yao et al., 2018). In addition, atmospheric carbon nanoparticles may carry toxic trace elements and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH), which can have complex, synergistic impacts on health, resulting in respiratory and cardiovascular issues (Murr et al., 2004; Ali et al., 2018).

Ice nucleation is another important characteristic of carbonaceous aerosol. Conversions of liquid water to ice have to be started by an ice nucleation event, which is mostly induced by a heterogeneous ice nucleating particle. The particles that initiate ice nucleation are known to be inorganic, relatively large and hydrophilic in nature. Literature shows that ice nucleation by carbonaceous aerosol is very uncertain, with some reporting as very effective at nucleating ice (Lohmann, 2002) and others reporting that carbonaceous aerosol has the very-low ice-nucleating ability (Cozic et al., 2008). Oxidation impacts the nanostructure and hydrophilicity of carbonaceous particles, transforming their ice nucleation ability (Lupi and Molinero, 2014), and hydrophilic carbon nanoparticles are effective in ice nucleation (Whale et al., 2015).

Carbon capture, using different methods such as activated carbon, biochar, carbon nanotube, graphene oxide, and activated sludge (Ahmed et al., 2019; Sepehri and Sarrafzadeh, 2018), is considered to be a key in reducing carbon emissions into the atmosphere from combustion of fossil fuel and biomass and in minimizing global climate impact. The capture of carbon emitted in the particulate form to the atmosphere, such as black carbon, has great significance to reduce their effect on climate. Soot collected from specific sources such as diesel, petrol, and natural gas has been used to create carbon nanoparticles such as graphene nanosheets and carbon dots (CDs), showing possible utilization in the degradation of hazardous chemicals and biosensing application (Tripathi et al., 2016; Tian et al., 2009). The soot collected from a specific source is lesser complex in their characteristics compared to carbonaceous aerosol. Carbonaceous aerosol contains carbon nanoparticles emitted from many diffuse sources. This malevolent carbonaceous aerosol is an unrealized carbon source for the production of precious carbon nanoparticles. Atmospheric hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which is a strong oxidizing agent, occurs in the atmosphere and has significant impacts on the ambient air quality as reported by various researchers (Zuo and Hoigne, 1992; Jackson and Hewitt, 1999). Complementarily this atmospheric H2O2 may likely bring in the alternation of carbonaceous aerosol characteristics. There is a significant potential of oxidation and formation of carbon nanoparticles from the carbonaceous aerosol in the atmosphere by H2O2, inducing more toxicity and, thus, leading to harmful health effects such as cardiovascular diseases. The aim of the present study was a laboratory experiment on the oxidation of atmospheric carbonaceous aerosol using an H2O2 reagent which revealed the formation of surface-functionalized hydrophilic carbon nanodots.

Aerosol samples (PM10) were collected on the CSIR-NEIST campus Jorhat, Northeast India, (Lat: 26.734016°N, Long: 94.160373° E) (from Supplementary Material Fig. S1) in the month of January and February 2018 alternate days (rainy days were avoided) for the duration of 7 h per day. Sampling was carried out on pre-conditioned (in an airtight desiccator for 24 h) glass microfiber filters (Whatman GF/A 8 × 10 ins sheet) using a respirable dust sampler (APM460 NL, Envirotech, India) at a flow rate of 1.2 m3 per minute. The filters were re-conditioned for another 24 prior to subsequent analysis. Mass concentrations of PM10 were determined by using the gravimetric method calculating the mass difference between the pre- and post-weighted filter. The standards guidelines national ambient air quality standards (NationalAmbient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), 2011) of the Central Pollution Control Board of India (CPCB) were followed for sampling and handling of filter papers.

A total of eight representative samples (J-03, J-07, J-11, J-15, J-29, F-01, F-03, F-17, F-25, and F-27) were taken considering the monthly average mass concentration of PM10 during the sampling period, and were extracted by rinsing the exposed part of the filter paper with distilled water in a borosilicate beaker followed by ultrasonic treatment at a frequency of 40 kHz in a Bench-top Ultrasonic Cleaner (Power-Sonic 520) at ambient temperature and pressure for 30 min. The extracted aerosol sample was then filtered by Whatman 41 (filtrate-A). H2O2 (30%) was added drop-wise (in 1:9 ratio) to the filtrate-A stirring with a magnetic stirrer at 50 rpm under room temperature and pressure, the solution was kept for 24 h prior to ultrasonic treatment for another 30 min. The H2O2 treated sample was then filtered by 0.22-μm membrane filters using a vacuum pump to reduce mineral impurities (filtrate-B) and to recover water-soluble carbon particles. The pH of the filtrates was measured with Eutech PC 700 pH meter.

A few drops of the extract and filtrate-A was placed on glass Petri dishes, dried in hot air oven at around 30 °C, and examined with a Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) system (ZEISS SIGMA, Carl ZEISS Microscopy (Germany), accelerating voltage: 0.5–30 kV) integrated with an Oxford energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis and EDS mapping were carried out by putting 10 μL of the filtrates on carbon-coated copper grids (HR-TEM: Joel JEM-2100 (Japan), resolution: 1.9 to 1.4 Å, accelerating voltage: 60–200 kV in 50 V steps). The FE-SEM and HR-TEM images were processed using the ImageJ software (version 1.47). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded in transmittance mode with 4 cm−1 spectral resolution using FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR Affinity-1, Shimadzu, Japan) and IR solution software. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd., UK) was performed in an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) conditions (≤5 × 10-10mbar) with an Al Kα X-ray source and a monochromator. The deconvolution of the XPS results was carried out using a Thermo Advantage 5.976 program. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded in a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Specord-200, Analytikjena AG, Germany). Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluorlolog-3, Horiba Ltd., Japan).

Aerosol optical depth (AOD) is based on the fact that the particles change the way the atmosphere reflects and absorbs visible and infrared light. For considering the regional aerosol characteristics, average monthly AOD data were retrieved from the NASA’s Terra/Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) satellite and plotted on Google Earth. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) HYbrid Single-Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model was used in the current study to show the motion of the air parcel. Five days backward trajectories of an air parcel at 300, 500, and 1000 m above ground level (AGL) were computed to determine the source regions. Meteorological data were obtained from the Meteorology station of Geoscience and Technology Division, CSIR-NEIST, Jorhat (India), and the wind rose was plotted using WRPlot view (Lakes environmental).

The mass concentration of aerosol (PM10) in the area during sampling time varied between 34.7 μg/m3 to 183 μg/m3 with an average concentration of 98.3 ± 8.63 μg/m3, slightly less than the CPCB national ambient air quality standards. On certain days the daily average concentration exceeded the permissible limits (from Supplementary Material Fig. S2). The maximum and minimum loading was possibily due to local biomass burning emission and weather conditions. Mass concentration was found to be decreased on the day after a rainfall. The aerosol concentration generally is found to be high during the winter season and maximum concentration occurs during the month of January to February according to the AOD data (from Supplementary Material Fig. S3). Average concentration (PM10) was less than the CPCB permissible limit but almost two times higher than the World Health Organization (WHO) (50 μg/m3) limit, indicating the necessity of scientific interest about aerosol pollution in the region. The region may face even higher concentration in the near future due to increasing local anthropogenic emissions and also transboundary movements of aerosol particles.

The aerosol samples were extracted for performing analytical characterization. The pH of the extract was in the range of 4.5–6. The extraction gives water-insoluble carbonaceous particles which on FE-SEM analysis reveal the presence of carbon-rich soot particles holding few other minerals such as Ca, K, and Na (from Supplementary Material Figs. S2a,b,c,d). FE-SEM-Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) of the particles reveals constituent of a heterogeneous mixture (Morillas et al., 2018) of elements like Fe, Mg, Al, Si, S, and Cl (from Supplementary Material Fig. S4). FE-SEM and HR-TEM analysis of filtrate-A shows the presence of aggregates of soot particles (Fig. 1 a and b), which are mostly freshly produced from biomass combustion or/and fossil fuel combustion. High-magnification HR-TEM analysis reveals that the soot particles possess a disorganized core and outer graphitic layers arrangement (Fig. 1c).

The soot aggregates of filtrate-A become water-soluble after reaction with an oxidizing agent (H2O2) and ultrasound treatment for 30 min depicting dispersed nanoparticles with particle sizes less than 10 nm in diameter (Fig. 2 a). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern indicates that the particles have a crystalline structure. Under high-resolution TEM the carbon nanoparticles show lattice planes with a spacing of 0.220 nm (Fig. 2b), which corresponds to (110) in-plane lattice of graphite (Tang et al., 2012). Carbon nanoparticles show the graphitic structure as well as the amorphous structure. EDS reveals that the particles are mostly composed of carbon with a few other elements such as Fe and Ca (Fig. 2c) as reported in literature (Morillas et al., 2019). EDS elemental mapping analysis (Fig. 2e–f and from Supplementary Material Fig. S5) clearly shows the distribution of different elements C, O, Fe, and Ca revealing high carbon content.

FT-IR characterization of the filtrate-B was carried out to identify the various chemical composition and surface states. The FT-IR spectra show the presence of functional groups CC, SO, C–O, CO, C–H, and O–H vibration modes, as depicted in Fig. 3 a. A wide absorption peak at about 3435 cm−1, corresponding to O–H vibration bonds and a weak peak at about 2945 cm−1 corresponding to the C–H vibration bond has been observed. The sharp absorption peaks at about 1631 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, 116 cm−1, and 618 cm−1 corresponding to CO, SO, C–O, and CC group respectively were found. The surface compositions of the samples were studied by XPS which depicts C and O as the dominant elements. High-resolution scanning shows that the C1S spectra split into three main peaks located at 284 eV (C–C sp2), 285.8 eV (C–O), and 287.93 eV (CO), and O1S spectra split into two main peaks located at 531.5 eV (C–O) and 532.7 eV (CO) (Fig. 3b and c). The XPS reveals that atmospheric carbonaceous aerosol contains sp2 carbon with functional groups such as carboxyl and carbonyl groups.

When observed under a UV lamp (at 360 nm) (Fig. 3d UV) the samples show bright blue photoluminescence and under fluorescence spectroscopy, it shows excitation-dependent emission spectra with enhanced intensities on rising excitation wavelength (Fig. 3d PL). The UV–visible spectrum shows absorption peak at 235 nm (Fig. 3d Abs.) related to the π-π* electronic transition of the sp2 hybrid orbital graphitic carbon (Tian et al., 2010). The electronic and spectroscopic properties of the samples are found to be similar in characteristics of CDs as reported elsewhere (Wang and Hu, 2014; Dimos, 2016). This implies the possibility of continuous mobilization of carbon nanoparticles such as CDs to the atmospheric carbonaceous aerosol and their significance to human health and climate. It was reported that exposure to the high concentration of atmospheric carbon nanoparticles can induce inflammation, hypersensitivity, cytotoxicity, and autoimmunity reactions (Morimoto et al., 2013; Lategan et al., 2018). Due to an uncontrolled environment, various metal impurities, surface functional groups and reactive oxygen species (ROS) are present in the atmospheric carbon nanoparticles which increases their toxicity (Lai et al., 2016). EDS shows a high concentration of iron (Fe) and calcium (Ca) is associated with the carbon nanoparticles (Fig. 2c). Metal iron impurities in carbon nanoparticles can induce cytotoxic and genotoxic effects (Aldieri et al., 2013).

On the other hand, the atmospheric carbonaceous aerosol may be a potential source for producing carbon nanoparticles such as CDs. In an urban atmosphere with high vehicular emission and a high concentration of carbonaceous aerosol, there is potential to develop methods for the production of carbon nanoparticles. Carbon nanoparticles are generally synthesized from various carbon sources by adopting different processes such as arc-discharge, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, laser ablation, and ultrasonic synthesis which involves carbonization. Carbonaceous aerosol, on the other hand, is particulate pollutant having sized up to the nanometer scale requires no carbonization. From our experiment, it is clear that carbon nanoparticle i.e. CDs can easily be produced from carbonaceous aerosol by low power ultrasonic-assisted simple oxidation with H2O2. Although the carbon nanoparticle obtained from carbonaceous aerosol may not be suitable for all-round applications as that of engineered products due to purity and toxicity concern, the process may provide value addition to hazardous pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible oxidation of atmospheric carbon nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Carbonaceous aerosols mainly fresh soot particles emitted from combustion sources are hydrophobic, water-insoluble and remain unchanged in their structures. The formation of carbon nanoparticles in the combustion emissions and their possible oxidation by atmospheric hydrogen peroxide (either gas-phase or aqueous) can take place producing surface functionalization hydrophilic carbon nanoparticles (Fig. 4 ). In our laboratory study, we observed the formation of CDs from atmospheric aerosol (PM10) due to the oxidation by H2O2. The interaction of carbon nanoparticles with atmospheric H2O2 may have significance on climate as oxidation influences their ice nucleation properties (Whale et al., 2015). The interaction may also affect human health as oxidation increases the toxicity of carbon nanoparticles (Lai et al., 2016).

Terra/MODIS AOD depicts that aerosol concentration increases from September–October and becomes the highest in the month of January and February in the North-eastern Region and ceases till June due to the arrival of monsoon (from Supplementary Material Fig. S6). The wind rose plotted from the local weather station data shows winds mostly from Northeast and Southeast direction with a maximum speed of 2 m/s during the sampling period (from Supplementary Material Fig. S1). Five days HYSPLIT back-trajectories at 300, 500, and 1000 m AGL shows that air parcel travels towards the study region mostly from the local area indicating local emission of the particulates in the region (from Supplementary Material Fig. S7). The NER is a highly forest-covered region of the country with more than 65% forest cover of its total geographical area (Forest Cover, 2017), has significant biomass burning activity with high fire frequency (Venkataraman et al., 2006) which may also contribute to the high concentration of carbonaceous aerosol. Other important sources may be vehicular emission and coal-fired brick kilns from the nearby areas.

Atmospheric aerosols-derived carbon nanodots containing impurities such as Fe and Ca are prominent in aerosol samples collected in Northeast India, which can have a significant impact on health and the environment and require further knowledge to determine their long-term effects. The study revealed the existence of carbon nanoparticles in the atmospheric aerosol which is reactive and toxic due to the formation of ROS and surface functional groups. The presence of carbon nanoparticles in the carbonaceous aerosol may be a new perspective for the recovery of high-value carbon dots from an atmospheric aerosol. Further study is needed to evaluate the nature and role of surface functional groups in the atmospheric carbon nanoparticles to predict their effects on climate and human health. The formation and oxidation of carbon nanoparticles could possibly influence the ice/cloud nucleation processes. These carbon nanoparticles with surface functional group may also be one of the reasons of toxicity and PMs related cardiovascular diseases. Contrarily, the formation of carbon nanoparticles in carbonaceous aerosol such as carbon nanodots might be appreciated as an opportunity for using it as a carbon source for CDs production. Mostly in urban areas where PM concentration is very high with high carbon content capturing of particulate carbon can significantly improve the urban air quality.
Shahadev Rabha: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft; Binoy K Saikia: Conceptualization; Methodology; Visualization: Writing-review & editing; Supervision

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Director, CSIR-NEIST (Jorhat) is thankfully acknowledged for showing his keen interest to this environmental research. Special thanks to Dr. Tonkeswar Das for his analytical assistance during the study. The funding from MoEFCC is duly acknowledged (NCAP-GPP-325). The authors also acknowledged the analytical facility received from SAIF (CSIR-NEIST), Jorhat. Authors would like to express their gratitude to the esteemed reviewers for their encouraging comments to improve the revision.
